question: A three-phase motor has a Table 44 full load current of 50 A. A non-time-delay fuse is sized at 300 percent (Table 29). What is the maximum value before rounding?
explanation: A non-time-delay fuse is sized at 300 percent, so 50 x 3 equals 150 A. 125 A reflects 250 percent and 100 A reflects 200 percent. 175 A reflects 350 percent, which is above the standard multiplier.

question: What does a two-pole three-phase motor running on 60 Hz indicate about its synchronous speed compared with a four-pole motor?

options.A: It runs at one quarter the synchronous speed.
options.B: It runs at half the synchronous speed.
options.C: It runs at twice the synchronous speed.
options.D: It runs at the same synchronous speed.

explanation: Fewer poles give a higher synchronous speed, so a two-pole motor runs at 3600 rpm versus 1800 rpm for a four-pole, which is twice as fast. It is not half or one quarter. The speeds are not the same.

question: A 5 HP three-phase motor at 575 V has a Table 44 full load current of 6.1 A. The overload is set at 125 percent. What is the trip current?
explanation: Overload at 125 percent of 6.1 A equals 7.625 A. 7.015 A applies 115 percent and 6.1 A applies no factor. 9.15 A applies 150 percent, which exceeds the overload range.

question: A three-phase motor has a Table 44 full load current of 12 A. A time-delay fuse is sized at 175 percent (Table 29). What is the maximum value before rounding?
explanation: A time-delay fuse is sized at 175 percent, so 12 x 1.75 equals 21 A. 24 A reflects 200 percent and 18 A reflects 150 percent. 30 A reflects 250 percent, which is the inverse time breaker multiplier.

question: You are connecting a new three-phase conveyor motor on a production floor. Before powering up, your journeyperson ticket requires you to verify phase rotation at the disconnect. A co-worker asks why this step matters before the motor runs for the first time. What is the main reason you check phase rotation?

options.A: To confirm the motor shaft will turn in the correct direction for the driven equipment.
options.B: To verify that supply voltage is balanced across all three phases before energizing.
options.C: To measure motor winding resistance and confirm insulation is within rated values.
options.D: To check that the overload relay trip current is set to the nameplate full-load amperage.

explanation: Phase rotation determines the direction of the rotating field and therefore the motor rotation direction, which must match the driven equipment. It does not set synchronous speed or adjust slip. It does not change the power factor to unity.

question: Which motor type is most commonly selected for general three-phase industrial drives because of its simple, rugged construction?
explanation: The squirrel-cage induction motor is rugged, low maintenance and widely used for general three-phase industrial drives. Wound-rotor and synchronous motors are chosen for special starting or power factor needs. DC series motors are used for specific high-torque applications, not general three-phase drives.

question: A 125 HP three-phase motor at 460 V has a Table 44 full load current of 156 A. What conductor ampacity is required at 125 percent?
explanation: Conductor ampacity is 125 percent of 156 A, which equals 195 A. 156 A omits the factor and 171.6 A applies only 110 percent. 234 A applies 150 percent, which is not the conductor factor.

question: A two-wire control scheme uses a single maintained contact device such as a float switch or thermostat to control a starter coil. Compared with three-wire control, what behaviour does two-wire control produce after a power interruption?

options.A: The motor restarts automatically when power returns.
options.B: The motor stays off until a start button is pressed.
options.C: The motor requires a manual reset of the overload.
options.D: The motor runs in the reverse direction briefly.

explanation: Two-wire control uses a maintained pilot device, so when power returns the still-closed device re-energizes the coil and the motor restarts on its own. Three-wire control loses its seal-in on power loss and needs a fresh start. Overload reset and reversing are unrelated to this restart behaviour.

question: After replacing a starter, an electrician finds the motor will not start at all. There is no hum from the contactor when the start button is pressed. Voltage is present at the line side of the contactor. Which control-side check should be made first?

options.A: Voltage across the coil while pressing start.
options.B: Continuity of the motor stator windings.
options.C: Insulation resistance of the feeder cable.
options.D: Rotation direction of the three phases.

explanation: No hum means the coil is not energizing, so the first check is whether control voltage actually reaches the coil when start is pressed. Stator continuity, feeder insulation, and rotation matter only after the contactor pulls in. Those power-side checks do not explain a dead control circuit.

question: A pilot light is wired across the starter coil to indicate the motor is running. If the pilot light is instead intended to show the motor is stopped, which contact would typically control it?
explanation: A stopped indicator uses a normally closed auxiliary that is closed when the starter is de-energized and opens when the motor runs. A normally open auxiliary lights only when running. The heater and start button do not provide a clean run-state indication.

question: A wye-delta starter requires the motor to have a specific number of leads brought out to the terminal box so both winding configurations can be made. How many motor leads are required?
explanation: A wye-delta starter needs all six winding ends, so the motor must have six leads brought out. A standard three-lead motor cannot be reconnected externally between wye and delta. Nine and twelve lead motors exist for other purposes but are not the requirement here.

question: A variable frequency drive controls the speed of an AC induction motor. To maintain motor torque across the speed range below base speed, the drive holds a particular ratio roughly constant. Which ratio is this?
explanation: A VFD holds the voltage-to-frequency ratio roughly constant below base speed so the magnetic flux and available torque stay nearly constant. The other ratios do not describe constant-flux control. Above base speed the drive cannot raise voltage further and torque falls off.

question: A PLC controls a conveyor. A photo-eye input is wired to a normally closed contact at the sensor but programmed with an examine-on instruction. When an object blocks the eye, the sensor contact opens. What does the program see for that rung input?

options.A: The input goes true and the rung closes.
options.B: The input goes false and the rung opens.
options.C: The output latches regardless of input.
options.D: The scan halts until the eye clears.

explanation: With a normally closed sensor contact, blocking the eye opens the field contact so the PLC input bit turns off, making the examine-on instruction false and the rung path opens. It does not turn the input true. The scan continues normally and the output is not latched by this alone.

question: A limit switch is used in a control circuit on a machine with moving parts. What does a limit switch primarily detect?

options.C: Physical position of a moving part.

explanation: A limit switch is a mechanical device that detects the position of a moving part when the part contacts its actuator. Liquid level is sensed by a float switch, pressure by a pressure switch, and temperature by a thermostat. Each pilot device matches a specific physical quantity.

question: A float switch controls a sump pump in the auto mode of a hand-off-auto circuit. The float is wired so a rising level closes its contact. Trace the action: the level rises and closes the float, energizing the coil. What stops the pump in this two-wire scheme?

options.A: The level drops and the float reopens.
options.B: The operator presses a stop button.
options.D: The overload relay trips on rising level.

explanation: In a two-wire float scheme the maintained float contact opens when the level falls, de-energizing the coil and stopping the pump. There is no stop button or seal-in in two-wire control. The overload responds to current, not to level.

question: A power factor correction capacitor is connected on the load side of a motor starter so it switches with the motor. Why is connecting the capacitor on the load side of the overload relay generally avoided?

options.B: The capacitor would not discharge safely.
options.C: The motor could not be reversed.
options.D: Capacitor current would affect overload sensing.

explanation: If the capacitor is on the load side of the overload relay its current is not seen the same way, and it can change the current the overload measures, leading to incorrect protection. Connecting it on the line side of the overload keeps motor current sensing accurate. Discharge, reversing, and the control transformer are not the core issue here.

question: A timing relay is used in a wye-delta starter to control the transition from the start configuration to the run configuration. Which type of timing function is normally required for this transition?

options.B: Off-delay timing after the stop button.
options.D: On-delay timing after the start contactor closes.

explanation: An on-delay timer starts counting when the start contactor energizes and transfers to delta after a set delay, allowing the motor to accelerate in wye first. Off-delay and repeat-cycle timing serve other functions. An instantaneous transfer would defeat the reduced-voltage purpose.

question: A line or one-line diagram and a ladder or schematic diagram serve different purposes. What does the ladder diagram primarily show that the one-line does not?

options.C: The physical layout of the panel.
options.D: The detailed control logic and connections.

explanation: A ladder diagram shows the detailed control logic with each device and its connections arranged between the supply rails. A one-line shows the simplified power path. Physical layout and conduit routing are given by wiring and installation drawings, not the ladder.

question: A manual motor starter differs from a magnetic motor starter in how the contacts are operated. How are the main contacts of a manual starter operated?

options.A: By a solid-state thyristor switching circuit.
options.B: By an electromagnetic coil pulling in an armature.
options.C: By hand through a mechanical toggle or button.
options.D: By a pneumatic actuator on a timer.

explanation: A manual starter is operated by hand using a mechanical toggle or pushbutton that directly moves the contacts. A magnetic starter uses an electromagnetic coil to pull in the contacts. Solid-state and pneumatic actuation are not how a manual starter works.

question: A manual motor starter for a small single-phase motor still includes thermal overload protection. What advantage does a magnetic starter offer that a manual starter cannot?

options.C: Remote and automatic control of the motor.

explanation: A magnetic starter can be controlled remotely and automatically through pilot devices and its coil, which a hand-operated manual starter cannot do. Current rating depends on the device, not the type. Both can include overloads, and short-circuit protection is provided separately by fuses or breakers.

question: An ambient-compensated overload relay is selected for a motor located in a hot environment near the starter. What problem does ambient compensation solve?

options.A: Nuisance tripping from heat around the relay.
options.B: Slow response to a true motor overload.
options.C: Coil chatter at low control voltage.
options.D: Phase loss on the three-phase supply.

explanation: Ambient-compensated overloads adjust for the surrounding temperature so heat around the relay does not cause nuisance tripping unrelated to motor current. They do not slow response to genuine overloads. Coil chatter and phase loss are separate problems handled by other means.

question: An electronic overload relay can provide phase-loss protection that a basic thermal overload may not. Why is phase-loss protection valuable for a three-phase motor?

options.B: Phase loss reverses the motor rotation.
options.C: Phase loss raises the control voltage.

explanation: If one phase is lost the motor single-phases and the remaining windings carry excessive current, overheating quickly. Phase loss does not reverse rotation or raise control voltage. It worsens, not improves, motor operation and power factor.

question: An analog input module on a PLC reads a signal from a level transmitter. Which of the following is a common analog signal range used in industrial control?
explanation: A four to twenty milliamp current loop is a common analog signal range because it is noise resistant and detects broken wires by reading zero. Line voltages and discrete pulse counts are not analog transmitter signals. The current loop is the industry standard analog interface.

question: A normally open held-closed limit switch is used on a machine guard. The switch is held closed while the guard is in place and opens when the guard is removed. If wired in series with the start circuit, what happens when the guard is opened during running?

options.A: The overload relay resets the circuit.
options.B: The motor starts in reverse automatically.
options.C: The seal-in keeps the motor running.
options.D: The control circuit opens and the motor stops.

explanation: With the held-closed switch in series, removing the guard opens the contact, breaks the control circuit, and stops the motor. The seal-in cannot hold because the series path is broken. The switch does not cause reversing or overload reset.

question: A control circuit is fed from a separate control transformer rather than directly from two line phases. What safety and reliability advantage does the separate control transformer provide?

options.A: Elimination of the overload relay need.
options.B: Higher control voltage for faster operation.
options.C: Lower control voltage and isolation from line.
options.D: Direct connection of the neutral to ground.

explanation: A control transformer provides a lower, isolated control voltage that is safer to work on and decouples the control circuit from full line voltage. It does not raise control voltage or remove the overload requirement. Grounding arrangements are a separate consideration.

question: A sequencing system must start three conveyors in order with a delay between each, and stop them in reverse order. Which combination of control elements best implements the timed start sequence?

options.A: Only manual pushbuttons at each motor.
options.B: On-delay timing relays with interlocking auxiliary contacts.
options.C: A single two-wire float switch for all motors.
options.D: Power factor capacitors switched in steps.

explanation: On-delay timers stagger each conveyor's start while interlocking auxiliaries ensure each runs before the next, building the ordered timed sequence. Manual pushbuttons cannot guarantee timing or order. A float switch and capacitors have nothing to do with timed conveyor sequencing.

question: A remote-control circuit operating at 120 volts with overcurrent protection sized to the wiring is classified under the Code as which type?
explanation: A 120 volt control circuit exceeds Class 2 limits and relies on conventional overcurrent protection, making it a Class 1 circuit. Class 2 and Class 3 are power-limited and stay at low voltages. It is not an extra-low-voltage communication circuit because the voltage is well above the extra-low-voltage range.

question: In Canadian fire alarm work, which standard governs the installation of fire alarm systems?
explanation: CAN/ULC-S524 is the standard for the installation of fire alarm systems in Canada. S537 covers verification of installed systems, not installation. S561 and S139 address monitoring and recorder requirements rather than general installation.

question: A device that detects fire and sends a signal to the panel, such as a smoke detector or pull station, is termed what?
explanation: Initiating devices sense fire conditions or manual operation and signal the control panel. Notification appliances are outputs like horns and strobes, not inputs. A supervisory relay and an annunciator serve monitoring and display functions rather than initiation.

question: Which cable category is the minimum commonly specified for Gigabit Ethernet to 1000BASE-T over 100 metres?
explanation: Cat5e is the minimum recommended twisted-pair category rated for reliable Gigabit Ethernet to 100 metres. Cat3 and the older Cat5 lack the performance margins for 1000BASE-T. Coaxial RG-6 is for video distribution, not Ethernet data over twisted pair.

question: What is the maximum horizontal channel length for balanced twisted-pair structured cabling under TIA-568?
explanation: TIA-568 sets the maximum channel at 100 metres, comprising 90 metres permanent link plus patch and equipment cords. The 90 metres alone is the permanent link, not the full channel. The fibre and coaxial figures do not apply to balanced twisted-pair limits.

question: Which cable type uses a single central conductor surrounded by a dielectric and braided shield?
explanation: Coaxial cable has a central conductor, dielectric insulation, and a braided or foil shield sharing the same axis. Twisted pair uses paired insulated conductors twisted together. Fibre uses glass strands and ribbon cable uses flat parallel conductors.

question: In twisted-pair cabling, why are the conductors twisted together in pairs?

options.A: To increase the cable diameter for strength.
options.B: To raise the operating voltage of the pair.
options.C: To reduce the number of conductors needed.
options.D: To cancel electromagnetic interference and crosstalk.

explanation: Twisting balances the pair so external interference and crosstalk are largely cancelled. It is not done to add diameter or raise voltage. The twist does not change how many conductors are required.

question: What is the role of an annunciator panel in a fire alarm system?

options.A: To indicate the location and status of alarms and troubles.
options.B: To supply standby battery power to the panel.
options.C: To detect smoke at the main entrance.
options.D: To sound the building evacuation horns.

explanation: An annunciator displays which zones or devices are in alarm or trouble, helping responders locate events. It does not supply battery power or detect smoke itself. Sounding horns is the job of notification appliances, not the annunciator.

question: Building automation systems commonly use which type of network to coordinate HVAC, lighting, and access?

options.B: A low-voltage control network using protocols like BACnet.

explanation: Building automation uses low-voltage control networks and protocols such as BACnet to tie subsystems together. It does not run on a 600 volt feeder or the fire alarm loop. A coaxial television backbone is for video, not automation control.

question: What is the typical purpose of a riser-rated communication cable?

options.A: Use only in outdoor direct burial.
options.C: Use in vertical shafts running between floors.
options.D: Use only inside sealed conduit underground.

explanation: Riser-rated cable is intended for vertical runs between floors and resists fire spread up shafts. Direct burial and underground conduit need other ratings. Plenum spaces specifically require plenum-rated cable, not riser cable.

question: Which describes the correct relationship between CAN/ULC-S524 and CAN/ULC-S537 on a project?

options.A: S537 governs installation and S524 governs verification.
options.B: Both govern only annual inspection of old systems.
options.C: Both apply only to security systems.
options.D: S524 governs installation and S537 governs verification afterward.

explanation: S524 sets installation requirements, and S537 verification confirms the completed installation works correctly. The roles are not reversed, so the second option is wrong. Neither is limited to annual inspection of old systems or to security systems.

question: When grounding a communication system per Section 60, where should the bonding conductor connect?

options.A: To the nearest gas piping only.
options.B: To an isolated ground rod with no other connection.
options.C: To the building grounding electrode system.
options.D: To the neutral of a branch circuit at a receptacle.

explanation: The communication bonding conductor should connect to the building grounding electrode system to keep all grounds at a common potential. Bonding to gas piping alone or an isolated rod creates dangerous potential differences. Tapping a branch-circuit neutral is not an approved grounding method.

question: You need to size standby batteries for a fire alarm panel. What load profile must they support?

options.A: Only the alarm time with no standby allowance.
options.B: Continuous full alarm for one week.
options.D: Supervisory standby time plus the required alarm time.

explanation: Standby batteries must carry the supervisory load for the required standby period plus the full alarm load for the required alarm duration. Sizing for alarm time alone omits the long standby demand. A one-week alarm or lamps-only basis does not match the standard load calculation.

question: While installing a nurse call system, what must be confirmed about the call cancel function?

options.A: Any staff member can cancel from any station.
options.B: The call cancels automatically after ten seconds.
options.C: A call can only be cancelled at the patient location.
options.D: Cancellation is done at the fire alarm panel.

explanation: Nurse call systems require that a patient call be cancelled only at the originating patient location to confirm staff attended. Remote or automatic cancellation would clear calls without a visit. The fire alarm panel has no role in nurse call cancellation.

question: To document a completed fire alarm verification, which information must be recorded for each device?

options.B: Device type, location, and pass or fail test result.
options.D: The colour of the wall it is mounted on.

explanation: Verification records each device by type, location, and test result so the system status is traceable. Only an installer name is insufficient documentation. Camera ratings and wall colour are irrelevant to fire alarm verification.

question: Which practice ensures a survivable fire alarm circuit where required by code?

options.A: Use ordinary lamp cord stapled to studs.
options.B: Run it loose in the return-air plenum unrated.
options.C: Use the specified fire-rated or survivable wiring method.
options.D: Splice it into the lighting branch circuit.

explanation: Where survivability is required, the specified fire-rated wiring method maintains circuit integrity during a fire. Lamp cord, unrated plenum runs, and lighting-circuit splices do not provide survivability. Following the rated method preserves operation when it is needed most.

question: When separating power and Class 2 communication cables that run parallel for a long distance, what is the proper practice?

options.A: Bundle them with a single tie every metre.
options.B: Run them in the same compartment of a divided box.
options.C: Tape the Class 2 cable directly to the power cable.
options.D: Maintain the required clearance between the two systems.

explanation: Power and Class 2 cables must keep the required clearance to limit induced noise and prevent accidental energization. Bundling, sharing a compartment, or taping them together defeats separation. Maintaining clearance is the correct field practice.

question: You measure the design EOL value across a loop but the panel still shows open. What should you suspect?

options.A: A break between the panel and the point you measured.
options.B: The resistor is the wrong colour.

explanation: Reading the correct value downstream while the panel sees open means the break lies between the panel and your measuring point. Resistor colour does not affect the electrical value. Firmware and notification loading do not explain a localized open.

question: A maglock on an exit door fails to release during a fire alarm test. Which wiring error best explains this?

options.A: The card reader address is duplicated.
options.B: The release relay is not tied to the fire alarm output.
options.C: The camera is on the wrong network.

explanation: Failure to release on alarm usually means the lock release was never connected to the fire alarm signal. Card reader addressing, camera networking, and EOL sizing do not control maglock release on alarm. The integration wiring is the fault.

question: A new fibre link tests with high loss far above the budget. The cable passed continuity. What is the most likely cause?

options.C: A tight bend or dirty connector end-face causing loss.

explanation: Excess loss with good continuity usually comes from a macrobend or contaminated connector end-face. End-of-line resistors and notification circuits do not exist on a fibre link. Device addressing is not a fibre loss factor.

question: An apprentice must select a CSA-rated meter for measuring voltage on a 600V three-phase distribution panel at the service equipment. Which CAT rating provides the correct transient protection for this location?
explanation: CAT IV instruments are designed for the origin of the installation where transient energy is highest, so a CAT IV 1000V meter suits a 600V service panel. CAT II and CAT I are intended for receptacle-level and protected electronic circuits and lack the transient withstand for service work. A CAT III 600V meter suits distribution within a building but lacks the CAT IV margin required at the service entrance.

question: When establishing a lockout on a three-phase disconnect, after applying locks and tags the worker must verify the absence of voltage. What is the correct final step before beginning work?

options.A: Assume zero energy once the lock is applied.
options.B: Test the meter on a known live source before and after.
options.C: Measure only phase to ground on one leg.
options.D: Open the disconnect and start work immediately.

explanation: The live-dead-live test confirms the meter works by checking a known source before and after testing the circuit, proving the dead reading is valid. Assuming zero energy or starting work without verification can be fatal if the disconnect is mislabeled or backfed. Measuring only one leg to ground fails to confirm all phases and any phase-to-phase voltage are absent.

question: A rigging plan calls for lifting a transformer using a two-leg sling at sixty degrees from horizontal. Compared to a straight vertical pull, the tension in each sling leg will be approximately what factor?

options.A: About zero point five eight of the vertical leg load.
options.B: About equal to the vertical leg load.
options.C: About one point one five of the vertical leg load.
options.D: About two point zero of the vertical leg load.

explanation: At sixty degrees from horizontal the sling tension multiplier is roughly one point one five because tension equals the share of load divided by the sine of the angle. A factor of zero point five eight corresponds to a reduction seen only at very steep angles and is incorrect here. Equal tension occurs only with a true vertical lift, and a factor of two would require a very shallow thirty degree angle.

question: On a set of electrical drawings, a single line shows a circle with a wavy line inside it. What does this symbol most commonly represent on a single line diagram?

options.D: A generator or alternating current source.

explanation: A circle enclosing a sine wave is the standard symbol for an AC generator or alternator source on single line diagrams. A motor starter coil is shown as a plain circle with letter designations and a current transformer uses a coil drawn around a line. A surge arrester is drawn as an arrow or zigzag to ground, not a circle with a wave.

question: An electrician needs to torque a lug rated for two hundred seventy-five inch-pounds. The available torque wrench reads in newton-metres. Which value most closely matches the required torque setting?
explanation: One inch-pound equals about zero point one one three newton-metres, so two hundred seventy-five inch-pounds gives roughly thirty-one newton-metres. The value of twelve would result from confusing foot-pounds with inch-pounds, and sixty-two doubles the correct figure. A reading of ninety-five newton-metres far exceeds the lug rating and would risk cracking the connector.

question: A worker is exposed to an arc flash hazard with an incident energy of six calories per square centimetre at the working distance. Which arc-rated PPE category provides adequate protection for this exposure?

options.A: PPE category one rated at four.
options.B: PPE category two rated at eight.
options.D: A face shield with no body protection.

explanation: PPE category two garments carry an arc rating of at least eight calories per square centimetre, which exceeds the six exposure and is therefore adequate. Category one at four falls below the incident energy and would not protect the worker. Untreated cotton can ignite and a face shield alone leaves the body unprotected against the thermal hazard.

question: Before entering a manhole to splice underground cable, the crew must treat the space as a confined space. Which atmospheric test must be performed first in the testing sequence?
explanation: Oxygen content is tested first because both the meter sensors and worker safety depend on a normal oxygen level, and low oxygen can make other readings unreliable. Flammable gas is tested second and toxic gases third in the standard sequence. Carbon dioxide is one of several toxic or asphyxiant concerns checked after oxygen, not before.

question: An electrician tests the insulation resistance of a 600V motor feeder using a megohmmeter. What is the typical minimum acceptable reading before energizing a circuit of this voltage class?
explanation: A common field rule requires at least one megohm of insulation resistance, often stated as one megohm per kilovolt plus one, so a 600V circuit needs roughly one to two megohms minimum. Readings below one ohm or near one hundred ohms indicate a serious insulation fault and a near short. A fixed value of six hundred ohms confuses voltage rating with resistance and is far too low to be safe.

question: A clamp meter is used to read load current on a three-phase feeder. If the technician clamps around all three phase conductors at once on a balanced load, what reading will the meter show?

options.B: Three times the single phase current.
options.C: The line current of one phase.
options.D: The square root of three times one phase.

explanation: On a balanced three-phase set the instantaneous currents sum to zero, so clamping all three together produces a near-zero reading because the magnetic fields cancel. Clamping all three does not multiply or isolate a single phase current. The square root of three factor relates line and phase quantities in voltage and power, not the vector sum of balanced currents.

question: A step ladder is being used near energized equipment. To meet electrical safety practice for this work, what material rating should the ladder have?

options.D: Any grade rated for general use.

explanation: Fibreglass ladders are non-conductive and are required when working near energized electrical equipment to reduce shock and arc risk. Aluminum and steel ladders conduct electricity and create a serious hazard around live circuits. A general-use grade rating addresses load capacity but does not address the conductivity hazard near electrical work.

question: A worker installs cable tray at height on a commercial site. Above what working height does fall protection generally become required on most Canadian construction sites?

options.A: About ten metres above a lower level.
options.C: About three metres above a lower level.

explanation: Fall protection is generally required at about three metres above a lower level on most Canadian construction sites, though provincial rules vary slightly. A trigger of ten metres is far higher than the common requirement. Twenty metres is unrealistic and there is always a height that triggers fall protection, so claiming none does is wrong.

question: A single-phase 120/240V service supplies a dwelling with a calculated load of twenty-four kilowatts after demand factors. What minimum service conductor ampacity is required to carry this load at 240V?
explanation: Dividing twenty-four thousand watts by 240 volts gives one hundred amperes, which sets the minimum conductor ampacity for the service. Values of one hundred twenty-five and one hundred fifty would apply to larger calculated loads and overstate this requirement. A two hundred ampere conductor would be oversized for this load and not the minimum required.

question: A delta-wye transformer has a 600V delta primary and a 208V wye secondary. What is the line-to-neutral voltage available on the secondary of this transformer?
explanation: On a 208V wye secondary the line-to-neutral voltage equals 208 divided by the square root of three, which is about one hundred twenty volts. The 208 volt figure is the line-to-line voltage, not line-to-neutral. The 240 and 347 volt values belong to other system configurations and do not appear on a 208V wye secondary.

question: A seventy-five kVA single-phase transformer steps 4160V down to 240V. Ignoring losses, what is the approximate full-load current on the primary side of this transformer?
explanation: Primary current equals seventy-five thousand divided by 4160 volts, which gives about eighteen amperes. The value of three hundred twelve amperes is the secondary current at 240V, not the primary. Figures of thirty-one and one hundred eighty amperes do not match either winding for this transformer rating.

question: A three-phase 600V feeder delivers one hundred fifty kilowatts to a balanced load at a power factor of zero point eight five. What is the approximate line current drawn by this load?
explanation: Line current equals power divided by the product of the square root of three, line voltage, and power factor, giving about one hundred seventy amperes. The one hundred forty-four ampere figure omits the power factor and the two hundred four figure misplaces it. A value of two hundred fifty amperes overstates the current for this load and voltage.

question: Bonding of service equipment to the grounded neutral is accomplished by a main bonding jumper. Where in the system is this main bonding jumper permitted to connect neutral and ground?

options.B: At the load side of the first overload.

explanation: The main bonding jumper connects the neutral to the bonding system only at the service, establishing a single point that limits objectionable current on bonding conductors. Connecting neutral and ground at subpanels or branch devices creates parallel neutral paths and circulating current. Bonding on the load side of the first overload device defeats the single-point design required by the code.

question: A standby generator is connected to a building through a transfer switch. To prevent both the utility and generator from energizing the load at once, what feature must the transfer arrangement provide?

options.C: A single overcurrent device for both.

explanation: A transfer switch must provide interlocking so the load connects to only one source at a time, preventing backfeed onto the utility and protecting line workers. A shared neutral or single overcurrent device does not stop both sources from connecting at once. Paralleling switchgear is a specialized arrangement for synchronizing sources, not the default for a simple standby system.

question: A two hundred ampere dwelling service uses copper conductors. According to common CEC service sizing, what minimum copper conductor size is typically used for this service?
explanation: A two hundred ampere copper service conductor is commonly three aught AWG, which carries the required ampacity at the service rating. A number four and number two are far too small for two hundred amperes and would overheat. A two hundred fifty kcmil conductor exceeds the requirement and is larger than needed for a standard copper service.

question: An overcurrent device is marked with an interrupting rating of ten kiloamperes, but the available fault current at its location is twenty-two kiloamperes. What is the correct action for the installer?

options.A: Install the breaker since it trips on overload.
options.B: Add a second ten kiloampere breaker in parallel.
options.C: Use a breaker rated at least twenty-two kiloamperes interrupting.
options.D: Derate the breaker to half its trip rating.

explanation: The interrupting rating must equal or exceed the available fault current, so a device rated at least twenty-two kiloamperes is required where that fault is available. A ten kiloampere breaker can fail violently when interrupting twenty-two kiloamperes even though it trips on overload normally. Paralleling two breakers or derating the trip setting does not increase the interrupting capacity to a safe level.

question: A grounding electrode conductor connects the service to a driven ground rod. If a single rod does not achieve the required resistance to ground, what does the code typically require?

options.A: A larger diameter single rod only.
options.C: Use of a smaller electrode conductor.

explanation: When a single electrode does not meet the resistance requirement a supplemental electrode is installed and bonded to improve the connection to earth. Simply increasing rod diameter gives little improvement compared with adding a second electrode. Removing the bonding jumper or reducing the electrode conductor size would compromise the grounding system rather than help it.

question: A one hundred twelve point five kVA three-phase transformer has a 600V primary. What is the approximate full-load line current on the primary winding of this transformer?
explanation: Primary line current equals one hundred twelve thousand five hundred divided by the product of the square root of three and 600 volts, which gives about one hundred eight amperes. The values of one hundred fifty-six and one hundred eighty-eight would correspond to lower voltages or single-phase assumptions. A figure of two hundred seventy-one amperes is roughly the secondary current at 240V, not the 600V primary.

question: On a three-phase four-wire 120/208V system, harmonic currents from nonlinear loads can overload one conductor in particular. Which conductor is most affected by triplen harmonics?
explanation: Triplen harmonics from nonlinear loads add together in the neutral rather than cancelling, so the neutral can carry more current than the phase conductors. The phase conductors carry the fundamental balanced load and do not accumulate triplen currents. Bonding and grounding electrode conductors carry no normal load current and are unaffected by harmonic loading.

question: A wye-connected secondary supplies 347/600V. A lighting circuit is connected line-to-neutral on this system. What voltage is applied to that single lighting load?
explanation: On a 347/600V wye system the line-to-neutral voltage is three hundred forty-seven volts, which is the value applied to a line-to-neutral lighting load. The six hundred volt figure is the line-to-line voltage between two phases. Values of two hundred eight and two hundred forty volts belong to other system configurations and do not appear on this wye system.

question: A feeder overcurrent device is sized for a continuous load. For a continuous load the conductor and overcurrent device must be sized to what percentage of the continuous load current?
explanation: Continuous loads require the overcurrent device and conductor to be rated at not less than one hundred twenty-five percent of the continuous load so the device does not operate at its limit for hours. The eighty percent figure is the inverse derating sometimes applied to the device rating. Using one hundred percent ignores the continuous duty requirement and one hundred fifty percent overstates it.

question: Two twenty-five kVA single-phase transformers are connected in open delta to supply a small three-phase load. What is the approximate maximum three-phase capacity of this open delta bank?
explanation: An open delta bank delivers about eighty-six point six percent of the two transformer ratings combined, so two twenty-five kVA units give roughly forty-three kVA of three-phase capacity. The fifty kVA figure is the simple sum that ignores the open delta reduction. The values of twenty-five and seventy-five kVA do not represent the open delta output of this bank.

question: A standby generator with a switched neutral feeds a building. On a separately derived system supplied by this generator, where is the neutral-to-ground bond established?

options.B: At the generator as a separately derived source.

explanation: When the transfer switch opens the neutral, the generator becomes a separately derived source and its neutral is bonded to ground at the generator. Bonding only at the utility would leave the generator-fed system without a ground reference when separated. A generator system must be bonded somewhere, and bonding at every panel creates parallel neutral paths.

question: A generator is rated one hundred kilowatts at a power factor of zero point eight. What is the apparent power rating of this generator expressed in kilovolt-amperes?
explanation: Apparent power equals real power divided by power factor, so one hundred kilowatts divided by zero point eight gives one hundred twenty-five kVA. Multiplying instead of dividing yields the incorrect eighty kVA figure. The one hundred kVA and one hundred sixty kVA values do not match the relationship between the stated kilowatts and power factor.

question: A service entrance has a calculated demand load. The grounded neutral conductor of the service may be reduced in size based on what quantity?

options.A: The total connected load before demand.
options.C: The available fault current at the service.
options.D: The maximum unbalanced load on the neutral.

explanation: The neutral conductor is sized for the maximum unbalanced load it must carry, which is often less than the phase load, allowing a reduction. Using total connected load before demand would oversize the neutral unnecessarily. The largest phase conductor size and available fault current do not determine the neutral sizing for unbalance.

question: A three-phase 600V delta distribution system feeds a three-phase motor. If one phase conductor opens while the motor runs, what happens to that motor?

options.A: It single-phases and may overheat or stall.
options.B: It runs normally on two phases.
options.C: It speeds up due to reduced load.

explanation: Losing one phase causes the motor to single-phase, drawing excessive current that can overheat the windings or stall the motor. It cannot run normally because the rotating field is disrupted by the missing phase. The motor does not speed up or reverse direction from a lost phase.

question: A consumer service has multiple disconnecting means grouped at the service. The code limits how the supply may be fully de-energized. What is that maximum grouping limit commonly applied?

options.A: One disconnect only in all cases.
options.B: Up to six operations of the hand.

explanation: Service disconnecting means are grouped so the supply can be fully disconnected with no more than six operations of the hand. A single disconnect is common but not always required. Unlimited disconnects defeat the purpose of quick disconnection, and a fixed limit of exactly two is not the rule.

question: An equipment grounding fault occurs on a 600V system. The bonding path must have low enough impedance to do what during a fault condition?

options.A: Slow the fault current to a trickle.
options.B: Store the fault energy for later release.
options.C: Carry fault current to operate the overcurrent device.
options.D: Increase voltage on the bonding conductor.

explanation: The bonding path must have low impedance so it carries enough fault current to quickly operate the overcurrent device and clear the fault. A path that limits current to a trickle would prevent the breaker from tripping and leave equipment energized. Bonding conductors do not store energy, and a proper path keeps touch voltage low rather than raising it.

question: A thirty kVA single-phase 240V transformer secondary is protected by an overcurrent device. What is the approximate secondary full-load current used to size that protection?
explanation: Secondary current equals thirty thousand divided by 240 volts, which gives one hundred twenty-five amperes for sizing the overcurrent protection. The sixty-two ampere value would apply to a much smaller transformer. Figures of one hundred and one hundred forty-four amperes do not match the thirty kVA at 240V calculation.

question: A service mast extends well above the roof and supports a heavy overhead service span. What is the typical remedy to resist the lateral pull on the mast?

options.A: Install a guy wire or back brace.
options.D: Lower the point of attachment below the roofline.

explanation: A guy wire or back brace resists the lateral strain from the service drop on a tall mast and keeps it from bending. Thinner conduit would be weaker and bend more easily under load. Removing the weatherhead would expose conductors to weather, and the point of attachment must meet clearance heights so it cannot simply be lowered.

question: A fuse and a circuit breaker both protect against overcurrent. Which statement best describes a key operational difference between these two devices?

options.A: A fuse can be reset after it operates.
options.B: A fuse must be replaced after it operates.
options.C: A breaker melts a link to open the circuit.
options.D: A breaker provides no short circuit protection.

explanation: A fuse contains a metal element that melts and must be replaced after it clears a fault, while a breaker uses a mechanism that can be reset. A fuse cannot be reset, which rules out that option. A breaker does not melt a link and does provide short circuit protection within its interrupting rating.

question: A forty-five kVA three-phase transformer supplies a 208V wye secondary. What is the approximate full-load secondary line current of this transformer?
explanation: Secondary line current equals forty-five thousand divided by the product of the square root of three and 208 volts, which gives about one hundred twenty-five amperes. The one hundred eight ampere value would apply to a 240V secondary. Figures of one hundred fifty and two hundred sixteen amperes overstate the current for this transformer at 208V.

question: A 600V to 240V single-phase transformer has four hundred primary turns. To produce the 240V secondary, approximately how many turns must the secondary winding have?
explanation: The turns ratio equals the voltage ratio, so secondary turns equal four hundred times 240 divided by 600, which is one hundred sixty turns. Two hundred forty turns confuses the voltage value with a turns count. Four hundred turns would give a one-to-one ratio and one thousand turns would step the voltage up rather than down.

question: A grounding electrode at a building is supplemented by bonding to a metal water pipe. For the metal water pipe to serve as part of the grounding electrode system, what condition must it meet?

options.A: It must contact earth for the required length.
options.B: It must be plastic for safety.
options.C: It must be painted to prevent corrosion.
options.D: It must be isolated from the building steel.

explanation: A metallic water pipe electrode must be in direct contact with the earth for the minimum required length to serve as a grounding electrode. A plastic pipe cannot conduct and is not an electrode. Painting the pipe would insulate it from earth, and isolating it from building steel removes a bonding path rather than improving the electrode.

question: A 600V feeder supplies a panel ninety metres away carrying one hundred amperes, and the voltage drop is found to be excessive. What is the most direct corrective measure?
explanation: Increasing the conductor size lowers resistance and reduces voltage drop on the long feeder. Adding overcurrent devices does not change the conductor resistance or the voltage drop. Frequency does not affect resistive voltage drop in a meaningful way, and the bonding conductor carries no load current to correct.

question: A generator set is sized to start a large motor whose starting inrush is several times its running current. Why must the generator be sized larger than the running load alone?

options.A: To reduce fuel consumption at idle.
options.B: To allow the neutral to float.
options.C: To supply high starting current without excessive voltage dip.
options.D: To raise the system frequency during start.

explanation: The generator must supply the large motor starting current without the voltage dipping so far that the motor fails to start or other loads drop out. Oversizing is not about fuel economy at idle. Allowing the neutral to float and raising frequency are not reasons to oversize a generator for motor starting.

question: A Class three control circuit differs from a Class two circuit chiefly in that it may operate at what condition?

options.A: Lower voltage with no insulation needed.
options.C: Only direct current under all conditions.
options.D: Higher voltage with added shock protection requirements.

explanation: Class three permits higher voltage than Class two while imposing extra requirements to protect against shock at that higher level. It is not lower voltage or free of insulation. Class three power is still limited, and it is not restricted to direct current only.

question: Three number six copper conductors at ninety degree insulation share one raceway, where the ninety degree ampacity is sixty-five amperes. After applying a zero point eight derating factor for three to six conductors, what is the corrected ampacity?
explanation: Starting from the ninety degree value of sixty-five amperes and multiplying by the zero point eight grouping factor gives fifty-two amperes. The forty-four ampere figure would result from using a smaller starting value, and sixty-five amperes ignores the derating entirely. A value of seventy-five amperes is higher than the uncorrected rating and is not possible here.

question: A 120V branch circuit runs sixty metres one way to a load drawing sixteen amperes using number twelve copper at about six point six ohms per thousand metres. What is the approximate voltage drop on this circuit?
explanation: The total conductor length is one hundred twenty metres for the round trip, giving about zero point seven nine ohms, so sixteen amperes times that resistance is roughly thirteen volts. A value of five volts ignores the return conductor length. Figures of twenty-five and forty volts overstate the resistance or current for this circuit.

question: A junction box contains four number twelve conductors, the bonding conductors counted together as one, and a single device strap. Using two point three cubic inches per allowance, what minimum box volume is required?

options.A: About eleven point five cubic inches.
options.B: About eighteen point four cubic inches.
options.C: About sixteen point one cubic inches.

explanation: Four conductors plus one for all bonding conductors plus two for the device strap gives seven allowances, and seven times two point three cubic inches equals about sixteen point one cubic inches. Counting only the conductors would understate the fill near eleven cubic inches. Adding extra allowances incorrectly produces the larger eighteen and twenty-three cubic inch figures.

question: A number fourteen copper conductor at ninety degrees is rated fifteen amperes. Four conductors share a raceway needing a zero point eight derating, and a forty degree ambient needs a zero point nine one correction. What is the approximate corrected ampacity?
explanation: Multiplying fifteen amperes by zero point eight for grouping and then zero point nine one for ambient gives about eleven amperes corrected. The nine ampere figure would result from applying an extra reduction not present here. Using only one of the two factors leaves the value too high at thirteen or fifteen amperes.

question: Electrical metallic tubing is being installed outdoors and exposed to weather. Which characteristic makes a wiring method suitable for this damp or wet location?

options.B: Use of standard indoor set screw fittings.
options.C: A thinner wall for easier bending.

explanation: A wet location wiring method needs corrosion resistance and raintight fittings to keep water out and prevent rust. Standard indoor set screw fittings are not raintight and allow water entry. A thinner wall does not address moisture, and painting threads is not an approved method of sealing against water.

question: A receptacle is installed in a kitchen counter area within reach of a sink. Which protective device is required on this receptacle to guard against shock?

options.A: An arc fault circuit interrupter only.

explanation: A receptacle near a sink requires ground fault circuit interrupter protection to disconnect quickly if current leaks to ground through a person. An arc fault device protects against arcing faults but not the shock hazard from ground leakage. A surge device and thermal overload do not provide the personnel shock protection required near water.

question: Conductors enter a Class one Division one hazardous location where flammable gases may be present. What wiring practice prevents the passage of gases and flames through the conduit?

options.B: Open ventilation slots in the box.

explanation: Seal-off fittings filled with approved compound are required in Class one Division one locations to stop gases and flames from travelling through the conduit. Standard couplings provide no barrier to gas migration. Ventilation slots would let gases in, and plastic conduit is not suitable for these hazardous locations.

question: A run of number ten copper conductors at ninety degrees is rated forty amperes, but the terminations are rated seventy-five degrees where number ten is thirty-five amperes. What ampacity governs the final installation?

options.B: Fifty-five amperes from a larger column.
options.C: Thirty amperes from the sixty degree column.

explanation: The lowest temperature rating in the circuit governs, so the seventy-five degree termination value of thirty-five amperes limits the installation even though the conductor is rated ninety degrees. The forty ampere value cannot be used because the terminals are not rated for ninety degrees. The fifty-five ampere figure belongs to a larger conductor, and thirty amperes is the sixty degree value not applicable here.

question: A 240V circuit feeds a load and the maximum allowable voltage drop is three percent. What is the largest voltage drop in volts that keeps this circuit within the limit?
explanation: Three percent of 240 volts equals seven point two volts, which is the maximum allowable drop for this circuit. The three point six volt figure is three percent of 120 volts, not 240 volts. Values of twelve and twenty-four volts represent five and ten percent of the supply and exceed the three percent limit.

question: A raceway is being filled with multiple conductors. The CEC limits conduit fill for three or more conductors to what maximum percentage of the conduit cross-sectional area?
explanation: For three or more conductors the maximum conduit fill is forty percent of the cross-sectional area to allow heat dissipation and pulling. The fifty-three percent figure applies to a single conductor and thirty-one percent applies to two conductors. A value of sixty percent exceeds all the standard fill limits.

question: An aluminum conductor is terminated at a breaker lug. To prevent a high resistance connection over time at this aluminum termination, what practice is commonly required?

options.A: Leaving the connection loose for expansion.
options.B: Soldering the aluminum directly to the lug.
options.C: Use of antioxidant compound and proper torque.
options.D: Mixing copper and aluminum strands together.

explanation: Aluminum terminations require an antioxidant compound and correct torque to prevent oxide buildup and loosening that cause high resistance and heat. Leaving a connection loose invites arcing and overheating. Soldering aluminum is not standard practice, and mixing copper with aluminum can cause galvanic corrosion at the joint.

question: A box fill calculation must account for internal cable clamps inside the box. How are these internal cable clamps counted in the box fill volume calculation?

options.A: Not counted at all in the calculation.
options.C: Two allowances like the device strap.
options.D: One volume allowance based on the largest conductor.

explanation: All internal cable clamps together count as a single volume allowance based on the largest conductor entering the box. They are not ignored, so a zero count is wrong. They count once as a group rather than per clamp, and they are not the same as the two-allowance device strap.

question: A branch circuit feeds an electric heater at 240V drawing twenty amperes over fifty metres one way using number ten copper at three point nine ohms per thousand metres. What is the approximate voltage drop?
explanation: The round trip length is one hundred metres, giving about zero point three nine ohms, and twenty amperes times that resistance is roughly eight volts. Using only the one way length would give the lower four volt figure. Values of sixteen and twenty-four volts overstate the resistance or current for this run.

question: A number eight copper conductor is listed at fifty amperes in the seventy-five degree column. Six current-carrying conductors share the raceway, requiring a zero point eight grouping factor. What is the corrected ampacity?
explanation: Multiplying the seventy-five degree value of fifty amperes by the zero point eight grouping factor gives forty amperes corrected. The thirty-two ampere figure would apply a heavier reduction than required. The fifty and fifty-five ampere values ignore or misapply the grouping derating.

question: A flexible metal conduit is used to connect to a vibrating motor. What is the main reason for using flexible conduit at this connection point?

options.A: To increase the conduit fill capacity.
options.B: To eliminate the need for bonding.
options.C: To absorb vibration and allow final alignment.
options.D: To raise the conductor temperature rating.

explanation: Flexible conduit at a motor absorbs vibration and allows minor movement and alignment so rigid conduit is not stressed. It does not change the fill capacity of the run. Bonding is still required across flexible conduit, and the conduit type does not change the conductor temperature rating.

question: A duplex receptacle has the tab broken between the two terminals on the hot side. What wiring arrangement does breaking this tab permit on a kitchen split receptacle?

options.A: A shared neutral with no ground.
options.B: A series connection of both outlets.
options.C: A bonding bridge to the box.
options.D: Two separate circuits on the same yoke.

explanation: Breaking the tab between the hot terminals lets each half of the receptacle be fed by a separate circuit, which is how a split kitchen receptacle is wired. It does not create a no-ground shared neutral arrangement. The outlets are not in series, and the tab break has nothing to do with bonding to the box.

question: A circuit is installed in a Class two Division one location where combustible dust is present. What is the primary wiring concern in this type of hazardous location?

options.A: Preventing dust ignition from heat or arcing.
options.B: Allowing free air movement through boxes.

explanation: In a Class two dust location the main concern is preventing ignition of combustible dust by enclosing heat and arcing in dusttight equipment. Free air movement would let dust accumulate inside equipment and is undesirable. Maximizing fill and reducing insulation rating do not address the dust ignition hazard.

question: A number six copper conductor at ninety degrees is rated seventy-five amperes and supplies a continuous load. To comply with the continuous load rule, what is the maximum continuous load this conductor should carry?
explanation: For a continuous load the conductor must be rated at one hundred twenty-five percent of the load, so the load cannot exceed seventy-five divided by one point two five, which is about sixty amperes. Using the full seventy-five amperes ignores the continuous load multiplier. Figures of ninety-four and one hundred amperes exceed the conductor rating for continuous duty.

question: Two conductors are installed in a conduit. The maximum fill for exactly two conductors in a conduit under the CEC is what percentage of the cross-sectional area?
explanation: For exactly two conductors the maximum conduit fill is thirty-one percent of the cross-sectional area. The fifty-three percent figure applies to a single conductor and forty percent to three or more. A value of sixty percent exceeds all the standard fill percentages.

question: A conductor type rated for direct burial is being installed in a trench. Compared with conduit-installed conductors, why can directly buried cable often carry more current?

options.D: The earth provides better heat dissipation.

explanation: Direct burial allows the surrounding earth to dissipate heat from the cable, often permitting higher ampacity than the same conductors confined in a raceway. Burial does not change the insulation temperature rating of the conductor. The conductor resistance and the applied voltage are not reduced simply by placing the cable underground.

question: A switch loop is wired with a cable feeding power to a switch and back to a light. In a modern installation, what conductor must now be present at the switch box?

options.A: A neutral conductor at the switch.

explanation: Modern rules require a neutral conductor at the switch location so electronic switches and devices have a return path. A second bonding conductor is not the requirement being addressed. There is no need for a bare travelling conductor or a spare phase conductor in a simple switch loop.

question: A continuous run of conductors passes through several pull boxes. To limit conductor stress, the CEC restricts the number of equivalent bends between pull points to what maximum?
explanation: The total bends between pull points must not exceed three hundred sixty degrees so conductors can be pulled without excessive strain. Limits of one hundred eighty and two hundred seventy degrees are stricter than the code requires. A value of four hundred fifty degrees exceeds the allowed total and would make pulling difficult and damaging.

question: A number eight copper conductor in free air at ninety degrees has a higher ampacity than the same conductor in a raceway. What is the main reason for the difference?

options.B: Free air lowers the conductor resistance.
options.C: Free air allows better heat dissipation.

explanation: A conductor in free air dissipates heat more readily than one bundled in a raceway, so it can carry more current. The insulation thickness is the same for the conductor regardless of location. Free air does not lower resistance, and a raceway does not increase the applied voltage.

question: A thirty ampere branch circuit must use which minimum copper conductor size based on standard Table 2 ampacity at the sixty degree column?
explanation: At the sixty degree column number ten copper is rated thirty amperes, making it the minimum size for a thirty ampere circuit. A number fourteen is rated fifteen amperes and number twelve is rated twenty amperes, both too small. A number eight is rated higher than needed and is not the minimum required size.

question: A motor circuit uses number six copper rated ninety degrees but terminates on lugs marked seventy-five degrees only. A technician wants to use the ninety degree ampacity. Why is this not permitted?

options.A: The termination limits the usable temperature column.
options.B: The ninety degree rating applies only to aluminum.
options.C: The conductor must be re-rated as bare.
options.D: The ninety degree column does not exist for this size.

explanation: The usable ampacity is limited by the lowest rated component, so the seventy-five degree termination caps the column even though the conductor insulation is rated ninety degrees. The ninety degree rating applies to copper as well as aluminum, so that reason is wrong. The conductor is not re-rated as bare, and a ninety degree column does exist for this size.

question: A wet location box mounted outdoors requires drainage. What feature helps prevent water from accumulating inside this enclosure?

options.A: A vapour seal at the top only.
options.B: A weep hole at the lowest point.

explanation: A weep hole at the lowest point lets any water that enters drain out, preventing accumulation in the enclosure. A vapour seal at the top does not let water escape from the bottom. Foam packing traps moisture and an upward facing open knockout would let more water in.

question: A raceway carries eight current-carrying conductors, and the grouping derating factor for seven to nine conductors is zero point seven. If each conductor would otherwise carry thirty amperes, what is the corrected ampacity per conductor?
explanation: Multiplying thirty amperes by the zero point seven grouping factor for seven to nine conductors gives twenty-one amperes corrected per conductor. The eighteen ampere value would use a heavier reduction than required. The twenty-four and thirty ampere figures do not apply the correct derating factor.

question: A receptacle circuit in a bedroom of a dwelling requires protection against series and parallel arcing faults. Which device satisfies this requirement?
explanation: An arc fault circuit interrupter detects the signature of series and parallel arcing faults and disconnects to reduce fire risk. A ground fault device protects against shock from ground leakage, not arcing in the conductors. A standard breaker and a surge device do not detect arcing fault signatures.

question: Conductors are spliced inside a box using wire connectors. The completed splice must be what to remain code compliant?

options.D: Left outside the box for cooling.

explanation: Splices must remain accessible without damaging the building finish so they can be inspected and maintained. Burying a splice in a wall makes it inaccessible and is not permitted. Tape alone is not an approved connector, and splices must be contained inside an enclosure, not left outside.

question: A 120V circuit supplies a load seventy-five metres away. To keep voltage drop within limits, the electrician upsizes from number twelve to number ten copper. What is the main effect on voltage drop?

options.A: Voltage drop increases with the larger wire.
options.B: Voltage drop decreases due to lower resistance.
options.C: Voltage drop stays exactly the same.

explanation: A larger conductor has lower resistance, so the voltage drop decreases on the long run. Upsizing does not increase the drop, since resistance falls with greater cross section. The drop does not remain the same and cannot be eliminated entirely while current flows through any resistance.

question: A conventional fire alarm initiating circuit uses an end-of-line resistor. What is the principal purpose of that resistor on the loop?

options.A: To limit the current to the smoke detectors.
options.B: To boost the signal voltage at the last device.
options.C: To supervise the circuit for open-conductor faults.
options.D: To convert the loop into a Class two circuit.

explanation: The end-of-line resistor keeps a small supervisory current flowing so the panel detects a break in the wiring as a trouble condition. It does not limit detector current or boost the signal voltage. It also has no role in reclassifying the circuit type.

question: Which cable category is the minimum commonly specified for reliable Gigabit Ethernet over a one hundred metre run?
explanation: Category five e is the minimum twisted-pair category rated for reliable Gigabit Ethernet to one hundred metres. Category three and older category five lack the performance margins for that speed. Coaxial RG six is for video distribution, not Ethernet data over twisted pair.

question: A communication cable installer must bond a metallic cable sheath where it enters a building under Section 60. Where should the bonding connection be made?

options.A: To the building grounding electrode system at the entrance.
options.B: To the nearest gas piping only.
options.C: To an isolated rod with no other connection.
options.D: To a live ungrounded branch conductor.

explanation: The sheath should bond to the building grounding electrode system at the entrance so all grounds share a common potential. Bonding only to gas piping or an isolated rod creates dangerous potential differences. Connecting to a live conductor is hazardous and is never permitted.

question: When terminating a category six jack, what is the correct way to handle the conductor pairs to preserve performance?

options.A: Untwist at least fifty millimetres for easier handling.
options.B: Untwist the pairs as little as possible at the termination.
options.C: Strip and untwist the full jacket length.
options.D: Untwist freely since it has no effect.

explanation: Pairs must be untwisted as little as possible right up to the termination to maintain crosstalk performance. Untwisting fifty millimetres or stripping the full length degrades the link significantly. Untwisting always matters because it directly affects near-end crosstalk.

question: A maglock secures a required exit door in an access control system. For safe egress, how must this maglock be arranged?

options.A: It stays locked during a fire alarm condition.
options.B: It is powered only from the notification circuit.
options.C: It releases on fire alarm activation and on loss of power.
options.D: It keeps the request-to-exit device disabled.

explanation: A maglock on a required exit must release on fire alarm activation and on loss of power so occupants can leave. Keeping it locked during an alarm would trap people inside. Powering it from the notification circuit or disabling request-to-exit defeats safe egress.

question: A single continuous-duty three-phase motor has a code table full-load current of forty amperes, and the conductors must be sized at one hundred twenty-five percent. What minimum conductor ampacity is required?
explanation: Multiplying the forty ampere table full-load current by one point two five gives fifty amperes as the minimum conductor ampacity for a single continuous-duty motor. Using forty amperes ignores the one hundred twenty-five percent sizing rule. The sixty ampere value overstates the multiplier and thirty-two amperes is below the table value.

question: An induction motor runs at seventeen hundred fifty rpm on a sixty hertz supply with four poles, where synchronous speed is eighteen hundred rpm. What is the approximate slip of this motor?
explanation: Slip equals the difference between synchronous and actual speed divided by synchronous speed, so fifty divided by eighteen hundred is about two point eight percent. A value of one point four percent would correspond to half that speed difference. Figures of five and ten percent represent much larger speed differences than fifty rpm.

question: A motor overload relay protects against sustained overcurrent. For a motor with a service factor of one point one five, overloads are commonly set to what percentage of the motor full-load current?
explanation: Overload protection for a motor with a one point one five service factor is commonly set at up to one hundred twenty-five percent of full-load current to allow normal operation while protecting against sustained overload. Setting at one hundred percent would nuisance trip on normal duty. A one hundred fifteen percent setting is conservative and one hundred fifty percent is too high to protect the windings.

question: A variable frequency drive controls motor speed. Which two quantities does the drive adjust together to maintain roughly constant magnetic flux?
explanation: A drive varies voltage and frequency together in proportion to keep the magnetic flux roughly constant across the speed range. Adjusting current and resistance is not how a drive controls speed. Power factor and slip are results of operation, and phase rotation only sets direction rather than controlling speed.

question: A motor branch circuit short circuit and ground fault device is set well above the motor full-load current. Why is this higher setting allowed for the short circuit protection?

options.A: To protect against slow overloads instead.
options.B: To reduce the conductor size needed.
options.C: To eliminate the need for overloads.
options.D: To allow the high starting inrush current.

explanation: The short circuit and ground fault device is set above full-load current so it does not trip on the brief high inrush during starting. It is not intended to protect against slow overloads, which the overload relay handles. It does not reduce conductor size and does not replace the overload function.

question: Two motor starters are wired for forward and reverse so only one can run at a time. What control feature prevents both contactors from energizing together?
explanation: Interlocking between the two starters, both mechanical and electrical, prevents both contactors from closing at the same time and shorting the supply. A seal-in contact only latches a coil and does not prevent the opposite direction. A shared overload or a single start button does not provide the needed interlock between directions.

question: A three-phase motor draws thirty amperes at 600V with a power factor of zero point eight. What is the approximate real power consumed by this motor?
explanation: Real power equals the square root of three times voltage times current times power factor, which is about twenty-five kilowatts for this motor. Omitting the power factor would give a higher figure near thirty-one kilowatts. Values of eighteen and thirty-six kilowatts do not match this calculation.

question: A PLC reads an input from a normally open pushbutton. In ladder logic, how is this normally open physical input typically represented when the button is pressed?

options.B: The output ignores the input entirely.
options.C: The input instruction goes true and passes logic.
options.D: The rung opens to stop current flow.

explanation: When the physical normally open button is pressed the PLC input turns on and the examine-if-closed instruction goes true, allowing the rung logic to pass and energize the output. The input does not stay false when the button is pressed. The output responds to the input, and the rung completes rather than opening when logic is true.

question: A motor nameplate lists a full-load current that differs from the code table value. Which value is used to size the branch circuit conductors for the motor?

options.A: The nameplate current for conductor sizing.
options.B: The locked rotor current for conductor sizing.
options.C: The no-load current for conductor sizing.
options.D: The table full-load current for conductor sizing.

explanation: Branch circuit conductors are sized using the code table full-load current value rather than the nameplate. The nameplate current is used for overload protection but not for conductor sizing. Locked rotor and no-load currents are not used to size the running conductors.

question: A four-pole motor is reconnected as a two-pole motor on the same sixty hertz supply. What happens to its synchronous speed when the poles are reduced?

options.A: It doubles to about thirty-six hundred rpm.
options.B: It halves to about nine hundred rpm.
options.C: It stays at eighteen hundred rpm.
options.D: It drops to about twelve hundred rpm.

explanation: Synchronous speed is inversely proportional to the number of poles, so halving the poles from four to two doubles the speed from eighteen hundred to about thirty-six hundred rpm. Halving the speed to nine hundred rpm would require doubling the poles. The speed does not stay at eighteen hundred rpm or fall to twelve hundred rpm when poles are reduced.

question: A motor fails to start and hums loudly when the contactor closes, and measurements show voltage on only two of the three phases at the motor terminals. What is the most likely fault?

options.A: An over-voltage on all three phases.
options.B: An open phase or single-phasing condition.

explanation: Voltage on only two phases with a humming stalled motor indicates an open phase, leaving the motor single-phasing and unable to start. An over-voltage would show high readings on all three phases, not a missing one. A failed capacitor or wrong rotation would not remove voltage from one phase.

question: A motor branch circuit serves a single continuous-duty motor with a table full-load current of twenty-eight amperes. What is the minimum conductor ampacity required at one hundred twenty-five percent?
explanation: Multiplying twenty-eight amperes by one point two five gives thirty-five amperes as the minimum conductor ampacity for the single motor. Using twenty-eight amperes ignores the required factor. The forty-two ampere figure applies too high a multiplier and twenty-two amperes is below the table value.

question: A variable frequency drive controls a fan motor, which is a variable torque load. Why does reducing fan speed with the drive save significant energy?

options.B: Power stays constant at all speeds.
options.C: Power varies with the square root of speed.
options.D: Power varies roughly with the cube of speed.

explanation: On a variable torque load like a fan, the power required varies roughly with the cube of speed, so a modest speed reduction yields large energy savings. Power does not rise directly with speed on such loads. It does not stay constant, and it does not follow the square root of speed.

question: A magnetic motor starter uses a control transformer to step 600V down to 120V for the control circuit. What is the main reason for using this lower control voltage?

options.A: To improve safety and use standard control devices.
options.B: To increase the contactor coil size.
options.C: To raise the motor running speed.

explanation: A lower control voltage improves operator safety and allows the use of standard 120V control devices like pushbuttons and pilot lights. It does not increase the coil size of the contactor. Control voltage does not affect motor speed and does not eliminate the need for overload protection.

question: A motor draws excessive current and trips its overload after several minutes running under a heavy load, while the short circuit breaker does not trip. What does this behaviour indicate about the protection coordination?

options.A: The short circuit device has failed open.
